nativeRange.habitat: Still or slow-flowing freshwater habitats including lakes, ponds, streams, ditches, and oxbow channels. Plants root in soft sediments and often form dense floating mats near the surface.
nativeRange.biotope: Warm-temperate to subtropical South American and southeastern North American slow-water freshwater

decorPreferences: open background or midground space for dense stem groups

summary: Green Cabomba (Cabomba caroliniana) is a fast-growing background stem plant with delicate, fan-shaped foliage, widely kept in freshwater aquariums. It generally does best under high light with CO2 supplementation. It is listed as an invasive species and is banned or restricted in many jurisdictions.
careNotes:
Cabomba caroliniana is the species most commonly sold under the trade name 'Green Cabomba' (also 'Carolina Fanwort' or simply 'Cabomba') and is distinct from Cabomba aquatica (the larger 'Giant' or 'Yellow' Cabomba) and Cabomba furcata (Red Cabomba). Stems carry pairs of bright green, finely divided fan-shaped underwater leaves and can readily reach the surface in a mature tank; mature stem length is generally reported between 30 and 80 cm. High light output is broadly recommended for healthy growth, and CO2 supplementation is generally advised to keep the plant looking its best. Green Cabomba draws most of its nutrients from the water column, so regular liquid fertilisation — especially iron and trace elements — is generally advised; a nutrient-rich planted-tank substrate is helpful but less critical than consistent dissolved nutrient levels. It tends to do best around 22–26 °C in soft, slightly acidic water (pH around 6.5, gH 3–8) with gentle to moderate flow. The stems are notably brittle and have been reported to snap easily during handling or in strong current. The plant is also sensitive to sudden swings in temperature, pH, or hardness, and has been known to melt back if parameters shift abruptly. Best appearance is achieved when planted in dense groups in the midground or background.

Legal / invasive status: C. caroliniana is widely listed as an invasive aquatic weed and is regulated in multiple jurisdictions — it appears on the EU Invasive Alien Species list (Regulation 1143/2014, banned from sale and possession across the EU), is restricted as a noxious weed in several US states (including California, Connecticut, Maine, Massachusetts, New Hampshire, Vermont, and Washington), and is regulated in Australia. Aquarists are responsible for checking local regulations before purchase, and trimmings should never be released to outdoor waters — dispose of cuttings as garden waste or by drying or freezing them first.
breedingNotes: Propagated easily by terminal stem cuttings — take healthy tops roughly 4–12 cm long, strip the lowest leaves and replant gently into the substrate; roots commonly develop within about a week under stable conditions. Lateral side-shoots from mature stems can also be separated and replanted. Fragments have been reported to remain viable free-floating for several weeks, which is one mechanism behind the species' invasive spread. Seeds exist but are rarely used in aquaria.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied Tropica primary did not return a species page for Cabomba caroliniana — Tropica's catalogue carries only Cabomba aquatica (item 015 BDT). Per the playbook fallback, the secondary source list was walked to find replacement primaries. Tropica is recorded here as the manifest-designated primary but supplied no species-specific data; all numeric fields are sourced from the additional[] entries below.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Manifest-supplied secondary (https://buceplant.com/products/cabomba-caroliniana) returned 404; Buce Plant does not currently list a dedicated C. caroliniana product page. Replacement sources used instead per the playbook fallback procedure.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (aquariumbreeder.com)
additional.notes: Primary substitute. Used for family (Cabombaceae), temperature (20–28 °C), pH (6.0–7.5), gH (3–8), high-light recommendation (≈30–40 PAR), CO2 recommendation, fast growth rate (~2.5 cm/day), brittleness note, water-column feeding habit, and invasive-status (US state bans, EU listing) notes.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range, temperature (68–82 °F = 20–28 °C), pH (6.0–7.5), background placement, propagation by cuttings, intermediate-to-advanced care level, and invasive listing in the US/EU/Australia. Calls CO2 'required' for best growth.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked midground-to-background placement, fast growth, temperature 72–82 °F, substrate tolerance (sand, gravel, planted substrates), liquid fertilisation, and the brittleness/clean-water preference. Rates light demand as medium, CO2 as beneficial-but-not-required.

additional.name: AquariumPlants.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult height (30–80 cm), native range (southern Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay, northeast Argentina, southern/eastern USA), and the 6–8 week viability of free-floating fragments.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Listed as an invasive aquatic plant in multiple jurisdictions — banned from sale and possession across the EU (Regulation 1143/2014 IAS list), restricted in several US states (CA, CT, ME, MA, NH, VT, WA) and regulated in Australia. Check local rules before purchase and never release trimmings to natural waters.
